front: What is a quantifier in logic, and what are the two primary types?
back: A quantifier expresses the extent to which a predicate is true over a range of elements. The two primary types are the universal quantifier (∀, 'for all') and the existential quantifier (∃, 'there exists').

front: Express the following statement using quantifiers: 'Every student in the class has taken a course in mathematics.'
back: Let the domain be students in the class and P(x) be 'x has taken a course in mathematics.' The statement is: ∀x P(x)

front: What is the logical negation of the universally quantified statement ∀x P(x)?
back: The negation is: ¬(∀x P(x)) ≡ ∃x ¬P(x). This means 'It is not true that P(x) for all x' is logically equivalent to 'There exists an x for which P(x) is false.'

front: What is the logical negation of the existentially quantified statement ∃x Q(x)?
back: The negation is: ¬(∃x Q(x)) ≡ ∀x ¬Q(x). This means 'It is not true that there exists an x for which Q(x) is true' is logically equivalent to 'For all x, Q(x) is false.'

front: What does it mean for a variable to be a 'bound variable' versus a 'free variable' in a logical expression?
back: A variable is bound if it is within the scope of a quantifier. A variable is free if it is not bound by any quantifier. For example, in ∃x P(x, y), x is bound and y is free.
